promises.body: Appropriate funds for ISS operations through FY2030. FY2026: $1.49 billion ($1.24B appropriations + $250M OBBBA reconciliation). Annual renewal required.
promises.note: FY2026 signed into law January 23, 2026 (P.L. 119-74). Congress rejected the Trump administration's proposed 24% NASA cut and 47% science cut. Bill includes language requiring NASA to spend 'no less than' allotted amounts — designed to prevent OMB from withholding funds. This is an ANNUAL promise — must be renewed for FY2027, FY2028, FY2029, and FY2030. The NASA Authorization Act of 2026, passed by Senate Commerce Committee March 2026, authorizes $24.7B for FY2026 and $25.3B for FY2027.

verification.source: Congressional Record; nasa.gov/budgets-plans-and-reports

promises.ref: ISS IGA Extension; White House announcement Dec 2021
promises.body: Operate the International Space Station through the end of 2030.
promises.note: U.S., Japan, Canada, and ESA member states committed to 2030. Authorization confirmed. Depends on annual appropriations (ISS-F01) being renewed each year. The NASA Authorization Act of 2026 further codifies this: NASA must maintain continuous U.S. human presence in orbit through and beyond ISS lifetime.

verification.source: White House statement; partner agency confirmations

promises.body: Continue ISS operations through the end of 2028. Participate in deorbit planning through 2030.
promises.note: Roscosmos Director General Dmitry Bakanov and NASA Acting Administrator Sean Duffy agreed July 31, 2025 at Cape Canaveral — first in-person meeting of space agency heads since 2018. Roscosmos committed to 2028, NOT 2030. Gap of 2 years between Roscosmos commitment (2028) and NASA/partner commitment (2030). Russia will participate in deorbit planning through 2030. Cross-flights (cosmonauts on Dragon, astronauts on Soyuz) continue.

verification.source: TASS; Reuters; NASA statements
verification.metric: Continued Roscosmos crew and vehicle presence

promises.body: Fund the U.S. Deorbit Vehicle development. Total NASA request: ~$1.5 billion (vehicle + launch). Contract value to SpaceX: up to $843 million. Launch procurement separate.
promises.note: Congress appropriated $0 for USDV in FY2024 despite a $180M request. FY2025 and FY2026 funding status for the USDV line item specifically needs verification. The overall ISS budget includes USDV but the specific allocation is unclear from public sources. The $843M contract is firm-fixed-price (SpaceX absorbs overruns), but the launch vehicle procurement has not been initiated.

verification.source: Congressional appropriations bills; NASA budget justifications

promises.body: Maintain dissimilar redundancy in crew transportation to ISS through two independent commercial providers (SpaceX Crew Dragon and Boeing Starliner).
promises.note: Single-provider dependency on SpaceX since September 2024. Boeing Starliner Crew Flight Test (June 2024) resulted in propulsion anomalies — thruster failures and helium leaks. NASA deemed it too risky to return crew aboard Starliner. Vehicle returned empty September 2024. Mission retroactively classified as Type A mishap (NASA's most severe category) in February 2026. Wilmore and Williams returned on SpaceX Dragon in March 2025 after 286 days. SpaceX has flown 18 successful Dragon missions. Boeing contract modified November 2025: reduced from 6 to 4 missions. Starliner-1 will be UNCREWED cargo-only, targeting no earlier than April 2026.

verification.source: NASA OIG reports; NASA CCP mission records
verification.metric: Number of certified crew transportation providers with demonstrated capability

promises.ref: SpaceX Commercial Crew Transportation Capability contract
promises.body: Provide crew rotation flights to ISS using Crew Dragon. Two rotations per year, 4 crew per flight, ~6-month missions.
promises.note: SpaceX is the sole operational crew provider. Crew-11 launched August 2025 (Cardman, Fincke, Yui, Platonov) but returned early January 15, 2026 due to medical evacuation — first in ISS history. Mike Fincke had a blood clot in internal jugular vein discovered via onboard ultrasound. Crew-12 (Meir, Hathaway, Adenot, Fedyaev) launched February 13, 2026 — accelerated from original schedule to minimize skeleton crew period. 18 successful Dragon crewed missions to date.

verification.source: NASA mission records; SpaceX launch manifests
verification.metric: Successful crew rotation flights per year

promises.body: Certify Starliner for crew transport. Fly uncrewed Starliner-1 cargo mission (targeting April 2026), then up to 3 crewed rotation missions through 2030.
promises.note: Boeing has exceeded its original $4.2B contract by over $2 billion in losses. Three test flights — all classified as partial failures or worse. OFT-1 (Dec 2019): partial failure. OFT-2 (May 2022): partial failure. CFT (June 2024): Type A mishap. Core issues: oxidizer valve temperature control, Teflon seal expansion restricting propellant flow, thruster thermal management. Starliner-1 (uncrewed) targeting NET April 2026. Contract reduced from 6 to 4 definitive missions.

verification.source: NASA CCP updates; Boeing press releases; NASA OIG
verification.metric: Successful mission completion rate; certification status

promises.body: Maintain life support systems (ECLSS) providing breathable atmosphere, water recycling, temperature/humidity control, and CO2 removal for up to 7 crew members continuously.
promises.note: Environmental Control and Life Support System (ECLSS) on U.S. segment operational. Water recovery rate ~90%. Continuous monitoring via telemetry. U.S. segment handles most ECLSS functions for the entire station.

verification.source: ISS telemetry; NASA On-Orbit Status Reports (daily)
verification.metric: Atmospheric composition, water recovery rate, temperature range

promises.body: Provide onboard medical capability including diagnostic tools, pharmaceutical supplies, and crew medical officer training. Maintain ability to perform medical evacuation within hours if required.
promises.note: Successfully demonstrated in January 2026 with the Crew-11 medical evacuation — first in ISS history. Mike Fincke's jugular vein blood clot was discovered via onboard portable ultrasound. NASA planned return over one week (not emergency deorbit). Crew-11 returned January 15, 2026 via Dragon Endeavour. NASA has standing agreement with U.S. military for global Dragon capsule recovery within 24 hours. Dragon can loiter in orbit up to 5 days post-undocking.

verification.source: NASA medical operations reports; Crew-11 post-mission review
verification.metric: Medical capability availability; evacuation capability readiness

promises.body: Monitor orbital debris and execute avoidance maneuvers (Pre-Determined Debris Avoidance Maneuvers — PDAMs) when collision probability exceeds threshold. Maintain crew shelter-in-place capability in docked vehicles.
promises.note: Active program. Multiple debris avoidance maneuvers conducted annually. Requires Russian segment propulsion for execution (depends on ISS-OO01). U.S. Space Command provides tracking data.

verification.source: U.S. Space Command tracking data; NASA mission control logs

promises.body: Deliver cargo to ISS via Dragon cargo spacecraft. Approximately 4-6 missions per year. Includes pressurized and unpressurized cargo, science experiments, crew supplies, and return of science samples to Earth.
promises.note: SpaceX CRS missions are the primary U.S. resupply vehicle. CRS-33 launched late 2025, included boost kit for orbital reboost testing. Dragon returns critical science and hardware to Earth — unique capability (Cygnus burns up on reentry). SpaceX also testing Dragon-based reboost capability as redundancy for Russian segment propulsion.

verification.source: NASA launch manifests; SpaceX mission records
verification.metric: Cargo mass delivered per mission; missions per year

promises.body: Deliver cargo to ISS via Cygnus spacecraft. Approximately 2 missions per year. Pressurized cargo only (Cygnus burns up on reentry — used for trash disposal).
promises.note: Cygnus provides secondary U.S. cargo capability. No return capability. Also serves as trash disposal — loaded with waste before destructive reentry. Northrop Grumman is also developing Cygnus autonomous docking for Starlab resupply.

verification.source: NASA launch manifests; Northrop Grumman mission records
verification.metric: Cargo mass delivered per mission; missions per year

promises.body: Deliver cargo and fuel to ISS via Progress spacecraft. Progress vehicles also provide orbital reboost capability and fuel reserves for attitude control and deorbit preparation.
promises.note: Progress remains critical for fuel delivery and reboost. However, Baikonur Cosmodrome Site 31 launch pad was damaged in a November 2025 Soyuz launch incident. NASA ISS Program Manager Dana Weigel stated fuel reserves need to reach required levels by early 2028 to begin orbital lowering in mid-2028. Delays to Progress launches directly impact deorbit timeline. SpaceX CRS Dragon with boost kit being tested as partial alternative for reboost.

verification.source: NASA ISS status reports; Roscosmos launch records
verification.metric: Progress missions per year; fuel delivered (kg)

promises.ref: Zvezda PrK leak monitoring, ongoing since 2019
promises.body: Maintain structural integrity of Russian segment modules, specifically resolve air leak in PrK transfer tunnel of Zvezda Service Module.
promises.note: Air leaks first detected September 2019 in PrK vestibule. Leak rate doubled in 2024 to ~1.7 kg/day — NASA elevated to highest risk rating (5/5 likelihood, 5/5 severity) in June 2024. By late 2025, pressure reportedly stabilized with no detectable air loss for several weeks. In June 2025, a 'new pressure signature' was detected — NASA concerned an interior wall leak may have formed. Delayed the Axiom-4 mission. Root causes: material fatigue from high-cycle vibration, possible welding defects from 1990s construction, 25+ years of thermal cycling. NASA and Roscosmos disagree on risk assessment: 'The Russians believe that continued operations are safe — but they can't prove that to our satisfaction.' — Bob Cabana, NASA ISS Advisory Committee chair.

verification.source: ISS pressure telemetry; NASA OIG report IG-24-020 (Sept 2024)
verification.metric: Air leak rate (kg/day); PrK pressure stability

promises.ref: ISS IGA; partner agency bilateral agreements
promises.body: Maintain U.S. segment modules, solar arrays, radiators, and external systems through EVA (spacewalks) and robotic maintenance using Canadarm2/Dextre.
promises.note: Regular EVAs for maintenance and upgrades. iROSA (ISS Roll-Out Solar Arrays) being installed to replace aging original arrays. Canadarm2 (CSA) and Dextre robotic systems operational. Crew-11 had a spacewalk scheduled (iROSA modification kit installation) that was cancelled due to the medical evacuation.

verification.source: NASA EVA logs; On-Orbit Status Reports

promises.body: Provide attitude control, orbital reboost, and debris avoidance maneuvering via Russian segment thrusters and docked Progress/Soyuz vehicles.
promises.note: Russian segment is PRIMARY for all attitude control and orbital adjustments. Dana Weigel: 'The Russian segment is prime for doing all of that. All of the attitude control, debris avoidance, anything we do with actively lowering is from the Russian segment.' No current full U.S. alternative exists, though SpaceX CRS Dragon boost testing provides partial redundancy. The deorbit plan relies on Russian segment for attitude control while USDV provides thrust. If Roscosmos departs in 2028 but deorbit extends past that date, a capability gap emerges.

verification.source: ISS telemetry; NASA mission control

promises.body: Maintain and operate Canadarm2 (Mobile Servicing System) and Dextre (Special Purpose Dexterous Manipulator) for station assembly, maintenance, visiting vehicle capture, and cargo transfer.
promises.note: Canadarm2 and Dextre are critical infrastructure. Used for capturing visiting cargo vehicles (Dragon, Cygnus), EVA support, payload installation, and ISS maintenance. Plans for Canadarm2 to potentially transfer to Axiom Station post-ISS retirement.

verification.source: NASA ISS telemetry; CSA operations reports

promises.body: Maintain and operate the Columbus laboratory module. Provide ESA astronauts for crew rotations. Support ISS operations through 2030.
promises.note: ESA committed to 2030. Columbus module operational. ESA astronaut Sophie Adenot part of Crew-12 (launched Feb 2026). ESA ceased most cooperation with Roscosmos after Ukraine invasion but maintained ISS participation.

verification.source: ESA mission reports; NASA crew manifests

promises.body: Maintain and operate the Kibo laboratory module and Exposed Facility. Provide JAXA astronauts for crew rotations. Support ISS operations through 2030.
promises.note: Japan was first to agree to 2030 extension (November 2022). Kibo module includes pressurized lab and external exposed facility for space environment experiments. JAXA astronaut Kimiya Yui was part of Crew-11 (medical evacuation, January 2026).

verification.source: JAXA mission reports; NASA crew manifests

promises.body: Design, develop, and deliver the U.S. Deorbit Vehicle (USDV) — a modified Dragon spacecraft with enhanced trunk (46 Draco thrusters, 16,000 kg propellant, 30,000+ kg total mass). Vehicle will be owned and operated by NASA.
promises.note: USDV based on Dragon with completely new trunk section — twice as long as standard, containing 30 deorbit thrusters + 16 attitude control thrusters. Firm-fixed-price contract (SpaceX absorbs overruns). ~50% of USDV is entirely new hardware. 100% of deorbit functionality is new. USDV planned to arrive mid-2029. Must launch ~18 months before final reentry. Docks to Harmony forward port. Launch vehicle procurement not yet initiated — NASA will procure separately ~3 years before launch.

verification.source: NASA CCP milestones; SpaceX development reports

promises.body: Execute controlled deorbit of ISS targeting uninhabited ocean area (South Pacific Uninhabited Area near Point Nemo). Debris footprint: ~2,000 km corridor. Surviving debris ranges from microwave-oven to sedan-sized pieces.
promises.note: Timeline per ISS Program Manager Dana Weigel: fuel reserves at required levels by early 2028 → begin orbital lowering mid-2028 (controlled drift using Russian segment) → USDV arrives mid-2029 → final crew departs at ~330 km altitude → station descends uncrewed to ~220 km → USDV performs series of burns over ~4 days → final reentry burn drives station into atmosphere → splashdown in ~2,000 km corridor. Total process: ~2-2.5 years. 925,000-pound structure moving at 17,100 mph. Russian segment provides attitude control throughout; USDV provides deorbit thrust.

promises.body: Ensure at least one commercial space station is operational before ISS is deorbited. Phase 2 awards planned for early 2026. Require demonstrated capability for crew, research, and commercial activities.
promises.note: NASA Authorization Act of 2026 codifies this: once a commercial station demonstrates for ONE FULL YEAR that it has sufficient capability, NASA is authorized to transfer operations and initiate ISS deorbit. Phase 2 awards expected early 2026. Trump administration modified CLD goals: from continuous crewed presence to accommodating 4-person crews for month-long missions (reduced scope). NASA hopes for 2-year overlap between CLD operations (2028) and ISS retirement (2030). Current CLD competitors: Vast Haven-1 (Q1 2027, limited); Axiom Station (first module 2027); Starlab (2029 via Starship); Orbital Reef (sluggish). STRUCTURAL CONFLICT: every dollar spent extending ISS is a dollar not spent on its replacement.

verification.source: NASA CLD program updates; GAO reports on CLD progress
verification.metric: Number of commercially operational stations; crew capacity

promises.body: Build and attach at least one habitable commercial module to ISS, then separate to form free-flying Axiom Station after ISS retirement.
promises.note: Axiom is the only CLD partner building flight hardware for a mature station. Axiom Hab One manufacturing underway, first module planned launch 2027 (originally 2026). Plan changed: PPTM (Payload Power Thermal Module) launches first, Hab-1 second. CONFLICT: USDV needs Harmony forward docking port — same port Axiom planned to use. Axiom and NASA working on resolution. Axiom has led 4 private astronaut missions (Ax-1 through Ax-4). Ax-4 delayed June 2025 due to Zvezda leak concerns. Axiom struggling with capital raising. This is the BOTTLENECK NODE in the transition chain.

verification.source: NASA CLD milestone reviews; Axiom Space announcements

promises.body: Develop and launch Starlab commercial space station — single-module station launched in one piece via SpaceX Starship. Target launch: 2029.
promises.note: Voyager Space + Airbus joint venture (Starlab Space LLC, formed Jan 2024). CEO: former astronaut Tim Kopra. Northrop Grumman joined consortium — providing Cygnus autonomous docking for resupply. Structural test article manufacturing planned for 2025. CDR targeted early 2026. Single-module = instant full capacity on arrival. Has international flights booked. Depends on Starship as launch vehicle — Starship's own development timeline introduces risk. Received $217M+ through SAA with NASA.

verification.source: Voyager Space / Starlab announcements; NASA CLD reviews

promises.body: Develop Orbital Reef commercial space station — multi-module 'mixed-use business park' in LEO. Launch via New Glenn.
promises.note: Blue Origin + Sierra Space led consortium. Received $130M SAA. Window tests and technology demos completed. 'Sluggish pace' compared to competitors. No public launch target date. Depends on New Glenn — which only completed first test flight recently. Least mature of the CLD competitors. Ranked last among CLD options by independent assessments.

verification.source: Blue Origin announcements; NASA CLD reviews

promises.ref: Vast Space unfunded SAA with NASA
promises.body: Launch Haven-1 single-module commercial space station (test platform, 4 crew, 2-week missions). Follow with Haven-2 (larger, modular) starting 2028.
promises.note: Dark horse competitor. Haven-1 purchased SpaceX Falcon 9 launch — originally targeted August 2025, now Q1 2027. Limited capability (4 crew, 2 weeks). Haven-2 (larger, modular, multiple launches via Falcon Heavy) planned first module 2028. Unfunded agreement with NASA — using SpaceX for launch, crew transport, training, and Starlink communications. Could be first commercial station in orbit if timeline holds.

verification.source: Vast Space announcements

promises.body: Maintain ISS as a microgravity research platform supporting the National Lab and partner agency research. Over 3,300 experiments conducted to date across biology, physics, materials science, and human physiology.
promises.note: Research operations ongoing. Key areas: protein crystal growth, tissue chips in space, 3D printing in microgravity, water purification systems, long-duration human health studies. Research depends on crew availability (reduced during skeleton crew periods like January 2026 after Crew-11 evacuation) and resupply cadence. Experiment upload/return logistics depend on Dragon (sample return capability) and Cygnus (no return).

verification.source: ISS National Lab annual reports; NASA science publications
